title: Chronicles: Temple, Kings, and Hope
objective: Deepen biblical-theological understanding of chronicles: temple, kings, and hope and its place in redemptive history.

required_passages.text: Selected key passages for this unit segment, read in full during lesson delivery.

segments.audio_script: Today we study chronicles: temple, kings, and hope and trace God's covenant faithfulness.
Listen to the assigned passage and note covenant themes, divine actions, and human response.
We place this text in its historical setting, canonical location, and literary flow.
We analyze theological meaning, covenant development, and connections to the wider biblical story.
Key themes include God's holiness, covenant faithfulness, judgment and mercy, and redemptive purpose.

Review question: What is the primary concern of Chronicles that distinguishes it from Samuel and Kings? Answer: Chronicles focuses on the temple, legitimate worship, and the Davidic line as the hope of the returning exiles.
segments.correct_answer: the temple, legitimate Davidic worship, and hope for the post-exilic community
acceptable_variants: worship centered on the temple and the davidic line
chronicles emphasizes the temple and priestly worship more than the political history of kings
the chronicler's concern is proper worship and the continuity of David's line

segments.audio_script: Carry this lesson into prayer, worship, and careful Scripture reading as we continue the course. Canonical integration for this unit also includes 1 Chronicles, 2 Chronicles, Esther, Job, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, Song of Solomon, Isaiah, Lamentations, Daniel, Hosea, Joel, Obadiah, Jonah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, and Zechariah.

vocabulary.definition: The author of 1-2 Chronicles, traditionally identified with Ezra; writing after the exile, he retells Israel's history from Adam to Cyrus's decree with emphasis on David, the temple, and proper worship
The proclamation of Cyrus king of Persia in 2 Chronicles 36:22-23 permitting the exiles to return and rebuild the temple; Chronicles ends with this word of hope pointing toward Ezra-Nehemiah
